14.text: Standard math library for attention scaling.

15.text: File path handling for the dataset.

16.text: Random number generation for sampling and shuffling.

17.text: time for benchmarking speculative vs autoregressive decoding speed.

19.text: PyTorch — this lab uses real tensors because we need two full models and speed benchmarks.

21.text: Functional API for softmax, ReLU, cross-entropy.

23.text: Fix Python's random seed for reproducible shuffling.

24.text: Fix PyTorch's random seed for reproducible weight initialization and sampling.

29.text: Builds the path to the dataset file relative to this script.

36.text: Load all names, one per line.

37.text: Shuffle to prevent memorizing alphabetical order.

40.text: Build character vocabulary from unique characters in all names.

48.text: Max sequence length for all models — names rarely exceed 16 characters.

51.text: RMSNorm: cheaper than LayerNorm, just rescales by inverse root-mean-square.

52.text: Dimension parameter unused but kept for API consistency.

54.text: Small epsilon prevents division by zero.

57.text: Scale x by inverse RMS — rsqrt computes 1/sqrt efficiently.

60.text: Causal (left-to-right) attention — both draft and target use the same architecture, just different sizes.

61.text: Constructor takes embedding dimension and number of heads.

71.text: Unpack batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension.

72.text: Project to queries and reshape: (B, T, C) → (B, n_head, T, head_dim). This splits the embedding into independent heads.

75.text: Scaled dot-product attention: Q·K^T / sqrt(d). The scaling prevents softmax from saturating in high dimensions.

76.text: Upper triangular mask: prevents each position from attending to future positions. This is what makes it causal.

77.text: Fill future positions with -inf so softmax gives them zero weight.

78.text: Softmax over the key dimension gives attention weights.

79.text: Weighted sum of values, then reshape back to (B, T, C).

80.text: Output projection mixes information across heads.

83.text: Feed-forward network: expand then contract, with nonlinearity in between.

86.text: Expand to 4x width — the MLP's "thinking space".

90.text: ReLU sandwiched between two linear layers.

93.text: A transformer block: attention + MLP with residual connections.

107.text: The full GPT model — used for both draft and target, just with different sizes.

108.text: Parameterized constructor: embedding size, heads, and layers are configurable for creating differently-sized models.

110.text: Token embedding: maps token IDs to dense vectors.

111.text: Position embedding: maps positions to dense vectors.

113.text: Stack of transformer blocks — the draft model gets 1, the target gets 2.

114.text: Language model head: projects hidden states to vocabulary logits.

115.text: Apply weight initialization to all submodules.

119.text: Only initialize Linear and Embedding layers.

120.text: Small Gaussian initialization (std=0.08) prevents exploding activations.

122.text: Forward pass: token IDs in, logits out.

126.text: Add token + position embeddings, then normalize.

129.text: Project to vocabulary logits — one score per token in the vocabulary.

135.text: CPU for this demo — the speedup story is cleaner on GPU where memory-boundedness is stark.

138.text: Draft model: 32-dim embeddings, 4 heads, 1 layer. Small and fast, but less accurate. It will propose tokens cheaply.

140.text: Target model: 64-dim embeddings, 4 heads, 2 layers. Better quality, but 4x slower per forward pass. This is the model whose distribution we want.

143.text: Shared training function — both models train the same way, just with different architectures.

144.text: Print model name and parameter count.

145.text: Adam optimizer with the standard hyperparameters.

148.text: Tokenize: BOS + characters + BOS (BOS doubles as EOS).

150.text: Input: all tokens except the last.

151.text: Target: all tokens except the first (shifted by one).

154.text: Zero out gradients from the previous step.

164.text: Create the draft model from config and move to device.

165.text: Create the target model — same architecture class, different size.

168.text: Train the target model separately — in production these would be pre-trained independently.

173.text: Temperature 0.5: moderate creativity. Lower values make output more deterministic.

176.text: Disable gradient computation for inference — saves memory and speeds things up.

177.text: Helper: run a model on tokens and return the probability distribution for every position.

179.text: Wrap tokens in a tensor, keeping only the last block_size tokens (sliding window).

180.text: Forward pass and apply temperature. Index [0] removes the batch dimension.

181.text: Softmax converts logits to probabilities. Returns (T, V) — one distribution per position.

187.text: Standard autoregressive generation: one token, one forward pass, repeat. This is the baseline we want to beat.

189.text: Start with just the BOS token.

192.text: Get probabilities for the last position only.

193.text: Sample one token from the distribution.

196.text: Append the new token and continue.

197.text: Return the generated sequence, stripping the initial BOS.

203.text: The heart of the lab: speculative decoding with draft model draft and target model target. K=4 means the draft model guesses 4 tokens ahead.

220.text: Statistics tracking: how many tokens drafted, accepted, and how many forward passes each model ran.

225.text: Copy current tokens — the draft model will extend this speculatively.

226.text: Track each drafted token and its probability under the draft model.

228.text: Stop if we've hit the length limit.

229.text: Break out of the draft loop.

230.text: Get draft model's next-token probabilities. This is cheap because the draft model is small.

232.text: Sample a token from the draft distribution.

233.text: Record the token and its draft probability q(x) — we'll need q(x) for the acceptance ratio.

236.text: Edge case: no tokens were drafted.

239.text: Record how many tokens the draft model proposed.

243.text: The key efficiency win: the target model scores ALL drafted positions in ONE forward pass. Autoregressive decoding would need K separate forward passes. Since inference is memory-bound (reading weights dominates), one pass costs almost the same as K.

244.text: Count target forward passes — should be much fewer than autoregressive.

247.text: Track how many consecutive drafts get accepted in this round.

248.text: Verify each drafted token against the target model's distribution.

251.text: Map draft position to target's output position.

254.text: Get the target model's probability p(x) for the exact token the draft proposed.

257.text: Zero draft probability means the draft model would never have picked this token — reject.

259.text: The acceptance probability: min(1, p(x)/q(x)). If the target likes this token at least as much as the draft, always accept. If the target likes it less, accept proportionally. This ratio is what guarantees the output distribution matches the target exactly.

261.text: Coin flip: accept with the computed probability.

263.text: Token accepted — add to the final sequence.

265.text: Special case: accepted token is BOS (end of sequence).

266.text: Remove BOS from the output and stop generating.

267.text: Update stats (don't count BOS as accepted).

268.text: Return the generated sequence and statistics.

271.text: Get the target's full distribution at this position.

272.text: Reconstruct the draft context up to the rejected position.

273.text: Re-run draft model to get its full distribution (needed for the correction term).

274.text: The rejection correction: max(0, p(x) - q(x)). This subtracts the draft's contribution, leaving only the "extra" probability mass that the target assigns. After normalization, sampling from this corrected distribution guarantees overall samples come from p(x).

276.text: If there's positive mass in the adjusted distribution...

277.text: Normalize to a proper probability distribution.

279.text: Fallback: if adjustment zeroes everything out, use the target's raw distribution.

285.text: After rejection, restart the draft loop from the new position. Don't try to reuse remaining drafts.

287.text: Python for-else: this runs if the loop completed without break — meaning ALL K tokens were accepted.

289.text: When all K drafts are accepted, we get a bonus: the target already computed the probability distribution for the position after the last draft.

290.text: Bounds check on the bonus position.

291.text: Get the target's distribution for the bonus position.

292.text: Sample a bonus token — a free extra token from the target with no additional cost.

293.text: Don't append BOS (that ends the sequence).

296.text: Update accepted count at end of round.

298.text: Return the completed sequence and statistics.

306.text: Switch draft model to evaluation mode (disables dropout if any).

307.text: Switch target model to evaluation mode.

311.text: Utility: time a generation function over n_samples runs.

317.text: Return samples and elapsed time for further analysis.

320.text: Benchmark 1: standard autoregressive decoding with the target model (the baseline).

321.text: One forward pass per token — the slowest approach.

326.text: List to collect results with stats.

328.text: Benchmark 2: speculative decoding (draft proposes, target verifies).

329.text: K=4: draft guesses 4 tokens per round.

330.text: Lambda collects both the tokens and the acceptance statistics from each generation.

336.text: Benchmark 3: draft-only autoregressive (for reference — fast but lower quality).

337.text: Uses the small draft model alone.

343.text: Acceptance rate: what fraction of draft tokens the target model approved. Higher means the draft model closely matches the target.

344.text: Average target forward passes per sample — should be much less than the average token count.

345.text: Average draft forward passes per sample.

347.text: Average tokens generated speculatively (should match autoregressive closely).

351.text: Compare forward pass counts — this is the efficiency win.

353.text: Wall-clock speedup ratio: how much faster speculative is.

354.text: Token counts should be similar — the distribution is mathematically identical.

379.text: Show actual generated names from each method.

386.text: Show the first 10 samples from each method.

387.text: Decode token IDs back to characters and print.
